answer:
**Iqbal does not name a specific historical figure as an exemplary ancestor in this chapter.** Instead, he refers collectively to the Muslim youth's "**forefathers**" and "**ancestors**" as the desert Arabs—"camel-herders" from "**the Arabian desert**"—who rose to create civilization and empire. He exemplifies their greatness by noting how "**that very nation**" "**crushed underfoot the crown upon the head of Darius**" (a reference to Persian kings symbolizing defeated empires, poetically attributed to early Muslim/Arab conquerors).[^1]

The chapter also alludes to the prophet **Jacob** (Yaqub) in a metaphorical couplet about lost Islamic knowledge now in Europe—like "**the light of his own eye**" (his son Joseph/Yusuf) "**brightens Zulaikha's gaze**"—but this is not presented as an ancestor in the same historical/exemplary sense.[^2]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 13 "Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam" ([paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462)).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 13 "Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam" ([paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462)).
